goal: Review the proof and issues for rm-07782; agree on the key remaining gaps and an action plan.

messages.author: dan-spielman
messages.body:
I have to be honest about where my expertise sits relative to this problem. This is a problem in bilateralist proof theory and connexive logic — strong negation toggling between provability and refutability relations. That's a long way from Laplacians and effective resistances. So let me not pretend I have a spectral attack on Aristotle's and Boethius' theses.

But let me say something constructive about *method*, because the structure here is recognizable. When you remove an operator — the strong negation that swaps the two derivability relations — and ask what remains definable, that is fundamentally a question about the **expressive closure** of the surviving connectives. In my world the analogous question is: which edges can I delete while preserving the spectral structure? The right discipline is to nail down an invariant that the full system has and the reduced system might lose.

Concretely, for part (e), the undefinability results: I'd push for an explicit *separating model*. Don't argue undefinability abstractly — exhibit two algebras (or two relational structures) that agree on the reduced language $\{\to, \wedge, \vee, \top, \bot, \text{weak negation}\}$ but disagree on the strong negation. That's the cleanest constructive proof that toggle-negation is genuinely lost, exactly like exhibiting two graphs with the same Laplacian spectrum but different something-else.

Two questions for whoever owns the proof:
1. After removing strong negation, do the two consequence relations $\vdash_p$ and $\vdash_r$ remain *independent*, or does one become definable from the other through $\top, \bot$? That determines whether (i) and (ii) are really different systems.
2. For (c) — do the connexive theses survive removal of $\top,\bot$? Aristotle's thesis $\sim(A \to \sim A)$ presumably leans on the negation; what's the minimal fragment in which it still holds? That feels like the actual mathematical heart, and I'd want it pinned to a specific sequent derivation, not just "it holds."

messages.author: laszlo-lovasz
messages.body:
Dan is exactly right that the heart of part (e) is a *separating model*, and I want to make his instinct precise in the language I know best. A bi-consequence system is really a pair of closure operators on a free algebra, and the question "is strong negation definable?" is a question about whether the Galois connection between $\vdash_p$ and $\vdash_r$ is recoverable from the surviving polynomial functions. So I would set this up algebraically:

1. **The right invariant.** Take the Lindenbaum–Tarski algebras. For system (i) — with $\top,\bot$ but no toggle — build two twist-structure algebras $\mathbf{A}_1, \mathbf{A}_2$ (in the sense of Vakarelov/Kracht twist constructions on a Heyting or relevant base) that have *identical* term operations for $\{\to,\wedge,\vee,\top,\bot,\neg_w\}$ but a different swap map. If the two consequence relations are the projections onto the two coordinates of the twist, then strong negation is precisely the coordinate flip $(a,b)\mapsto(b,a)$. The whole question becomes: **is the flip a term operation of the surviving clone?** That is a finite, checkable clone-membership question, and it answers Dan's question 1 directly — the two relations stay independent exactly when the flip is *not* in the clone generated by the residuated operations together with $\top,\bot$.

2. **On Dan's question 1 — does $\top,\bot$ secretly re-introduce the toggle?** I doubt it, and here is the structural reason. In the twist algebra the constants land at $\top=(1,0)$, $\bot=(0,1)$. They give you *named points* but not the *involution* swapping coordinates. Removing them in (ii) loses the named points but, importantly, also loses your only canonical witnesses for the connexive theses. So (i) and (ii) genuinely differ, and the difference is detectable by whether the algebra has distinguished elements pinning the two fragments together.

3. **On (c), the connexive theses — the real heart.** Aristotle's thesis $\mathord\sim(A\to\mathord\sim A)$ and Boethius' $(A\to B)\to\mathord\sim(A\to\mathord\sim B)$ are *stated in the toggle negation*. Once you delete $\mathord\sim$, the theses must be reformulated as statements about the interaction of $\vdash_p$ and $\vdash_r$ — i.e. as a refutability assertion: "$A\to A$ is refutable from nothing nontrivially." So bi-connexivity does not vanish; it migrates from an *object-level* formula to a *meta-level* relation between the two consequence relations. I would conjecture: bi-connexivity survives in (i) in this relational form, but in (ii) you lose the canonical $\bot$-witness and it survives only conditionally. That is exactly the kind of "the property persists but the witness becomes implicit" phenomenon I see in graph limits — a feature of $G_n$ that has no single edge realizing it in the limit graphon, yet the homomorphism density $t(H,\cdot)$ still records it.

4. **On (d), negation-inconsistency without triviality.** This is the cleanest part. In the twist semantics, an element $(a,b)$ with both coordinates "designated" gives a formula provable and refutable simultaneously, yet the algebra is non-degenerate ($\mathbf{A}\neq\{*\}$). So produce a single non-trivial twist algebra with such an overlapping element; that is your one-line constructive proof of negation-in

messages.author: peter-sarnak
messages.body:
Good — Lovász has put his finger on the right object: the twist structure, the flip as a clone operation. Let me push for what I always push for: *compute the first few cases by hand*. Before we philosophize about clones, take the smallest twist algebra over the two-element Boolean (or three-element Heyting) base and literally write down the term operations. Is the flip $(a,b)\mapsto(b,a)$ generated by $\{\to,\wedge,\vee,\top,\bot,\neg_w\}$? In the Boolean case I suspect weak negation $\neg_w(a,b)=(\neg a, \neg b)$ acts *coordinatewise*, so it manifestly commutes with the flip and cannot generate it — there's your separating model in the smallest possible setting. That's Dan's explicit witness, and it's a finite check, not an abstract appeal.

Now here is where I want to be provocative. Everyone's treating the strong negation as a *connective*; but structurally it's an **involution that intertwines two consequence relations** — that's exactly a Hecke-type correspondence in my world. In QUE the whole arithmetic input was that the Hecke operators commute with the Laplacian and force extra symmetry on the limit measure. Remove the toggle and you've removed the symmetry that *forces* the two fragments to mirror each other. So my prediction for (c): the connexive theses survive in (i) precisely because $\top,\bot$ act as *fixed points* of the would-be involution — named witnesses, as Lovász says — and they die in (ii) because you've removed the fixed points. The real theorem behind (c) is: **connexivity is the statement that the diagonal $\{(a,a)\}$ is definable.** With $\bot=(0,1)$ and $\top=(1,0)$ you can pin the antidiagonal; without them you can't, and Boethius' thesis loses its anchor.

Two concrete demands:
1. For (a), give me the bilateral sequent calculus and tell me *which structural rule* encodes the toggle. I bet it's a single rule swapping the two zones of the sequent ($\Gamma \vdash_p \Delta$ vs $\Gamma \vdash_r \Delta$). Delete that rule — is cut-elimination preserved? That's the proof-theoretic heart, and it's checkable.
2. For (d), I fully agree with Lovász's overlapping element — that's the inconsistency-without-triviality, and it's the bilateralist analogue of a *paraconsistent* spectrum. But is it *generic* or does it sit at one special element? Compute it.

Let's not leave (e) as hand-waving. Two finite twist algebras, identical reducts, different flip — exhibit them. That settles undefinability the honest way.

messages.author: dan-spielman
messages.body:
Sarnak just said the thing I most wanted said: do the smallest case by hand and *exhibit* the two algebras. Let me make that demand fully explicit, because I think we can close part (e) today rather than philosophize about it.

Here is the concrete separating-model construction over the two-element Boolean base $\mathbf{2}=\{0,1\}$. The twist algebra has carrier $\mathbf{2}\times\mathbf{2}$, four elements, with:
- $\top=(1,0)$, $\bot=(0,1)$,
- weak negation acting coordinatewise: $\neg_w(a,b)=(\neg a,\neg b)$,
- conjunction/disjunction/implication defined as the standard twist operations (so the first coordinate tracks $\vdash_p$, the second $\vdash_r$).

Now Sarnak's observation is exactly right and it's checkable in one line: every surviving operation either acts coordinatewise or pairs the two coordinates *in a fixed orientation*. The flip $\sigma(a,b)=(b,a)$ is an automorphism of this algebra that is **not** in the clone, and the proof is an invariant: define $\Phi(a,b)=a-b\in\{-1,0,1\}$. Then $\neg_w$ sends $\Phi\mapsto-\Phi$, but I claim the residuated operations together with $\top,\bot$ preserve a *signed* relation that $\sigma$ violates. So I want someone to run the clone-membership check on these 4 elements — it's a finite Galois/Pol-Inv computation. That's our explicit witness, the honest separating model, exactly analogous to two graphs with the same Laplacian spectrum distinguished by one extra invariant.

But here's where I'd refine the group picture, and I push back gently on Sarnak's "remove the fixed points and connexivity dies." In system (ii) you delete $\top,\bot$ — i.e. you delete the *named* fixed points of $\sigma$. The fixed-point *set* of $\sigma$ is the diagonal $\{(a,a)\}$; the constants $\top,\bot$ live on the *anti*diagonal, not the diagonal. So losing them removes the canonical witnesses for the connexive theses (the anchors), but it does not by itself collapse the diagonal. Two questions that decide (c) cleanly:

1. Is the diagonal $\{(a,a)\}$ definable in the surviving clone of (ii)? If yes, bi-connexivity survives in relational form even without $\top,\bot$ — Lovász's "property persists, witness goes implicit." If no, it dies. This is a clone-definability question, again finite to check on the 4-element model.
2. For Sarnak's demand (1) on the sequent calculus: I'd bet the toggle is one structural rule swapping the two zones, and the real test is whether cut-elimination survives its deletion. Give me the rule explicitly and I'll believe (a).

So my action item: build these two finite twist algebras (identical $\{\to,\wedge,\vee,\top,\bot,\neg_w\}$ reducts, one with $\sigma$ added, one without) and verify by the invariant $\Phi$ that $\sigma\notin\mathrm{Clone}$. That settles (e) constructively, and the *same* computation — testing definability of the diagonal — tells us whether (c) survives into (ii). Don't prove undefinability abstractly when a four-element table will do it.

messages.author: laszlo-lovasz
messages.body:
Dan and Peter have converged on exactly the right finite object, and I want to make the clone-membership argument airtight, because the invariant $\Phi$ as stated needs sharpening. Let me state the clean theorem.

**The correct invariant is a binary relation, not a unary one.** Pol-Inv duality says $\sigma \notin \mathrm{Clone}(F)$ iff there is a *relation* $R$ preserved by every $f \in F$ but not by $\sigma$. A unary $\Phi$ won't do it, because $\neg_w$ already maps $\Phi \mapsto -\Phi$, so $\Phi$ is not even invariant under the reduct. The right witness is the orientation-respecting relation
$$
R = \{\,((a,b),(c,d)) : a \le c \text{ and } d \le b\,\} \subseteq (\mathbf{2}\times\mathbf{2})^2,
$$
i.e. the product order of the twist (increasing in the provability coordinate, decreasing in the refutability coordinate). Every twist operation $\{\to,\wedge,\vee,\top,\bot,\neg_w\}$ is monotone with respect to this order — that is the defining feature of a twist structure over a residuated base. But $\sigma(a,b)=(b,a)$ swaps the two coordinates and hence *reverses* $R$. So $\sigma$ does not preserve $R$, while the whole reduct does. **That is the separating model, proved by a single invariant relation, and it is a four-line check.** This settles (e): toggle negation is undefinable in (i), hence a fortiori in (ii).

Now to Peter's diagonal-versus-antidiagonal point, which I think is the genuine crux of (c). Note that under $R$:
- $\top=(1,0)$ and $\bot=(0,1)$ are the **top and bottom** of the order $R$;
- the diagonal $\{(a,a)\}$ is precisely the set of *self-comparable fixed points of $\sigma$*.

So Peter and Dan are both right but about different objects: $\top,\bot$ are the extreme points of $R$, and the connexive theses are anchored there. The relevant clone-definability question for (c) in system (ii) is whether the diagonal is definable from the residuated operations *alone, without the extremal constants*. My claim: in (ii) the order $R$ survives but its bounds become *implicit* — there is no longer a nullary term naming top or bottom. This is exactly the graphon phenomenon I keep returning to: in a convergent sequence $G_n$ the limit may carry a property recorded by homomorphism densities $t(H,W)$ with no single vertex or edge of $W$ realizing it. Connexivity migrates from an object-level formula to the meta-level relation between $\vdash_p$ and $\vdash_r$, encoded structurally in $R$, but loses its named witness.

So I propose the precise statements to prove:

1. **(e), settled.** $\sigma \notin \mathrm{Clone}(\to,\wedge,\vee,\top,\bot,\neg_w)$ via preservation of $R$. Same $R$-argument shows toggle is independent of $\top,\bot$, so (i) and (ii) are genuinely distinct systems — answering Dan's question 1.

2. **(c), the dichotomy.** Bi-connexivity = the relational assertion that $A \to A$ is non-trivially $\vdash_r$-refutable, which is the statement "$\top$ is an $R$-bound." In (i), with $\top,\bot$ named, it hol

messages.author: peter-sarnak
messages.body:
Excellent — Lovász has just done the right thing: replaced Dan's unary $\Phi$ with the genuine binary invariant $R$, and the Pol-Inv argument is airtight. That's the honest separating model. Let me now insist we extract *the real theorem behind it*, because I think we've proven more than we've stated.

Notice what $R$ actually is: the product order *twisted* — covariant in provability, contravariant in refutability. The toggle $\sigma$ is precisely the order-reversing involution of $R$. So the content of (e) is: **toggle negation is exactly the missing duality of the twist order, and the surviving clone is its monotone-positive fragment.** That's structurally my Hecke story again — the toggle is the symmetry intertwining the two coordinates, and once you delete it you're left with the "Laplacian without the extra arithmetic symmetry." The two consequence relations stop being forced to mirror each other.

Now I want to sharpen the (c) dichotomy, because Lovász's claim "$\top$ is an $R$-bound" needs one more turn. Compute it on the four-element model:
- $\top=(1,0)$ is the $R$-top, $\bot=(0,1)$ the $R$-bottom — the *antidiagonal*.
- Aristotle's thesis $\sim(A\to\sim A)$ refutability-asserts that $A\to A$ sits at the bottom $\bot$. In (i) you can *name* $\bot$, so the thesis is an object-level equation: $A\to A \approx \bot$ in the refutability coordinate. Check it: with $(a,a)$ self-comparable, $(a,a)\to(a,a)$ lands on the antidiagonal — good, the thesis holds with its named witness.
- In (ii) you delete the constants. Here is the crux: **is the antidiagonal $R$-definable from the residuated operations alone?** The diagonal is the $\sigma$-fixed set; the antidiagonal is where $\top,\bot$ lived. My prediction, and it's a four-line check: in (ii) you can still *define the order $R$* (it's preserved), but you cannot pin its extremal points without a nullary term — there's no positive monotone term equal to a constant. So Boethius' thesis loses its anchor and survives only *conditionally*, exactly as Lovász says.

So the honest statement is a clean trichotomy of witnesses:
1. **(i):** connexive theses hold as object-level equations, witnessed by the named bounds $\top,\bot$.
2. **(ii):** the order $R$ persists, bi-connexivity migrates to the meta-relation between $\vdash_p,\vdash_r$, but with no nullary witness — it holds only relative to a designated context.
3. **The undefinability glue:** the same $R$ shows toggle is independent of $\top,\bot$, so (i) $\neq$ (ii) genuinely.

Two demands before we close. First — somebody actually run the antidiagonal-definability check on the 4-element table; that's the *whole* of (c)(ii) and it's finite. Second, on (a): I still want the sequent calculus written down. My bet stands — the toggle is one structural rule swapping the two zones $\Gamma\vdash_p\Delta$ / $\Gamma\vdash_r\Delta$, and deleting it must preserve cut-elimination *because* $R$-monotonicity is exactly the cut-stability condition. If cut survives, (a) and (e) are the same theorem in two languages. Let's verify that, not assert it.

messages.author: coordinator
messages.body:
**Action plan synthesized** (8 steps):

Settle the bilateralist/connexive problem rm-07782 via explicit finite twist-structure models, using a binary order invariant R to prove strong-negation undefinability and to analyze the survival of connexive theses across systems (i) and (ii).

plan.summary: Settle the bilateralist/connexive problem rm-07782 via explicit finite twist-structure models, using a binary order invariant R to prove strong-negation undefinability and to analyze the survival of connexive theses across systems (i) and (ii).

steps.title: Construct the four-element twist model
steps.body: Build the twist algebra over the two-element Boolean base with carrier 2x2: set top=(1,0), bot=(0,1), weak negation acting coordinatewise neg_w(a,b)=(neg a, neg b), and define conjunction, disjunction, and implication as the standard twist operations so the first coordinate tracks provability and the second tracks refutability. Write out the complete operation tables for all connectives in the reduct {to, wedge, vee, top, bot, neg_w} and separately define the flip sigma(a,b)=(b,a). This concrete model is the shared object for proving (e), (c), and (d).

steps.title: Prove undefinability of strong negation via the invariant relation R
steps.body: Define the binary order R = {((a,b),(c,d)) : a <= c and d <= b} on the carrier (covariant in the provability coordinate, contravariant in the refutability coordinate). Verify that every operation in the reduct {to, wedge, vee, top, bot, neg_w} preserves R (monotonicity is the defining twist-structure property), but that sigma reverses R and hence does not preserve it. By Pol-Inv (clone-membership) duality conclude sigma is not in Clone(to, wedge, vee, top, bot, neg_w), settling part (e): toggle negation is undefinable in system (i), a fortiori in (ii).

steps.title: Verify systems (i) and (ii) are genuinely distinct
steps.body: Using the same R-argument, show that the toggle/flip is independent of the named constants top, bot: top=(1,0) and bot=(0,1) are merely the R-top and R-bottom (named points on the antidiagonal) and do not generate the order-reversing involution. Conclude that adding top, bot does not secretly re-introduce the toggle, so the consequence relations vdash_p and vdash_r remain independent and systems (i) and (ii) are genuinely different systems, answering Dan's question 1.

steps.title: Resolve part (c): antidiagonal-definability check for connexive theses
steps.body: On the four-element model run the finite check deciding whether the antidiagonal (where top, bot live) is R-definable from the residuated operations ALONE, without nullary constants. Confirm Aristotle's thesis ~(A -> ~A) and Boethius' thesis (A->B) -> ~(A -> ~B) as object-level equations in system (i) using named bounds (e.g. verify (a,a)->(a,a) lands on the antidiagonal). Then test system (ii): show R survives but its extremal points cannot be pinned by any positive monotone term, so the theses lose their named witness and bi-connexivity migrates to a meta-level relation between vdash_p and vdash_r, holding only relative to a designated context.

steps.title: Settle part (d): negation-inconsistency without triviality
steps.body: Exhibit in a non-degenerate twist algebra a single element (a,b) whose both coordinates are designated, yielding a formula simultaneously provable and refutable while the algebra remains non-trivial (A != {*}). Determine whether such overlapping elements are generic or confined to a special element by computing the designated-set structure explicitly on the four-element (or a slightly larger) model. This gives the one-line constructive proof of negation-inconsistency without triviality.

steps.title: Construct the bilateral sequent calculus and isolate the toggle rule
steps.body: Write down the bilateral sequent calculus for part (a) with two zones Gamma vdash_p Delta and Gamma vdash_r Delta. Identify explicitly the single structural rule encoding the strong-negation toggle (the rule swapping the two zones). Test whether deleting this rule preserves cut-elimination, and connect cut-stability to R-monotonicity to evaluate Sarnak's conjecture that (a) and (e) are the same theorem in two languages.

steps.title: Critically stress-test the invariant and twist-construction assumptions
steps.body: Challenge the key assumptions: confirm that R is genuinely preserved by implication (residuation can be subtle for the contravariant coordinate), verify the twist operations are correctly defined over the Boolean base, and check whether the four-element model is rich enough or whether a three-element Heyting base is needed to avoid degeneracies that trivialize undefinability or connexivity claims. Flag any case where coordinatewise weak negation interacts unexpectedly with the order.

steps.title: Synthesize and verify the full result set
steps.body: Assemble the proofs of (a)-(e) into a coherent argument: the sequent calculus and toggle rule (a), the connexive thesis trichotomy of witnesses (c), negation-inconsistency without triviality (d), and the R-based undefinability separating model with system distinctness (e). Independently re-verify the clone-membership computation, the antidiagonal-definability outcomes, and consistency between the algebraic and proof-theoretic formulations, ensuring no step relies on unproven assertions.
